🩺 AI Pneumonia Detector– ML Pipeline¶

Submitted by: GH1041309

Course: M507 - Methods of Prediction

Submission Date: September 2025

1.Problem Statement¶

Business Context: Fictional Hospital MedScan¶

MedScan is a network of hospitals and diagnostic centres that looks at thousands of chest X-rays every week.
Radiologists must manually check each image — a task that is slow and can lead to fatigue.
Early and accurate detection of pneumonia is critical to shorten hospital stays and save lives.

To improve its service, MedScan wants an AI-based tool that automatically classifies incoming chest X-ray images as either Normal or Pneumonia.
This system will not replace radiologists but will triage cases:

  • 🟢 Normal scans can be reviewed later.
  • 🔴 Pneumonia scans are flagged immediately.

Underlying Business Problem¶

Every day, diagnostic centres face challenges in quickly and correctly identifying pneumonia cases.
With large image volumes, even expert radiologists can be overloaded.
Without an automated support system:

  • Diagnosis times increase.
  • High-risk cases are delayed.
  • Costs rise due to overtime and inefficiencies.

MedScan wants a robust image-classification pipeline to support its radiologists, improve patient outcomes and cut costs.

Why It Matters¶

For MedScan:-

  • Reduce workload on radiologists by pre-screening images.
  • Speed up diagnosis to improve patient care.
  • Save costs by reducing overtime and diagnostic delays.
  • Ensure consistent, reproducible classification of images.

For patients:-

  • faster, more reliable screening improves their safety and health outcomes.

Data Collection¶

I will use a public Kaggle dataset:

https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/paultimothymooney/chest-xray-pneumonia

This dataset contains over 5,800 labelled chest X-ray images (Normal vs. Pneumonia), already split into training, validation and test sets.

ML Task Formulation – Supervised Binary Image Classification Problem¶

(a) I will treat this task as a supervised binary classification problem because:

  • I have a labelled dataset where each image includes both the input (X-ray) and an output label (Normal or Pneumonia).
  • The goal is to predict a categorical outcome with two possible classes: Normal and Pneumonia.

(b) I will experiment with various deep learning models, including:

  • A baseline Convolutional Neural Network (CNN).
  • Transfer learning architectures such as MobileNetV2 and ResNet50.
  • Different hyperparameters (image size, learning rate, optimisers, augmentation strategies).

2.Data Exploration¶

In [2]:
# Imports
from zipfile import ZipFile
import pandas as pd
from PIL import Image
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import numpy as np
In [3]:
# Mount Drive
from google.colab import drive
drive.mount('/content/drive', force_remount=True)

import os, zipfile, cv2

zip_path = "/content/drive/MyDrive/Chest-Xray.zip"
extract_dir = "/content/extracted"
output_dir = "/content/extracted_equalized"
os.makedirs(output_dir, exist_ok=True)

# Extract ZIP
with zipfile.ZipFile(zip_path, 'r') as zip_ref:
    zip_ref.extractall(extract_dir)

# Equalize all images
def equalize_image(img):
    img = cv2.cvtColor(img, cv2.COLOR_RGB2GRAY)
    img = cv2.equalizeHist(img)
    return cv2.cvtColor(img, cv2.COLOR_GRAY2RGB)

for root, _, files in os.walk(extract_dir):
    for f in files:
        if f.lower().endswith((".png",".jpg",".jpeg")):
            img = cv2.imread(os.path.join(root,f))
            cv2.imwrite(os.path.join(output_dir,f), equalize_image(img))

print("✅ Extraction and processing done!")

Mounted at /content/drive
✅ Extraction and processing done!
In [4]:
#Count images and get size statistics
rows = []
with ZipFile(zip_path, 'r') as zipf:
    file_list = zipf.namelist()

    for subset in ["train", "val", "test"]:
        for label in ["NORMAL", "PNEUMONIA"]:
            imgs = [f for f in file_list if subset.lower() in f.lower()
                    and label.lower() in f.lower()
                    and f.lower().endswith((".jpeg",".jpg",".png"))]

            sizes = []
            for f in imgs:
                with zipf.open(f) as file:
                    img = Image.open(file)
                    sizes.append(img.size)

            rows.append({
                "subset": subset,
                "label": label,
                "count": len(imgs),
                "min_size": min(sizes) if sizes else None,
                "max_size": max(sizes) if sizes else None,
                "avg_width": sum([s[0] for s in sizes])/len(sizes) if sizes else None,
                "avg_height": sum([s[1] for s in sizes])/len(sizes) if sizes else None
            })

stats_df = pd.DataFrame(rows)
print("=== Dataset Summary ===")
print(stats_df)

In [6]:
# Display sample images (3 NORMAL + 3 PNEUMONIA)
with ZipFile(zip_path, 'r') as zipf:
    normal_imgs = [f for f in zipf.namelist() if 'normal' in f.lower() and f.lower().endswith(('.jpeg','.jpg','.png'))][:3]
    pneumonia_imgs = [f for f in zipf.namelist() if 'pneumonia' in f.lower() and f.lower().endswith(('.jpeg','.jpg','.png'))][:3]
    samples = normal_imgs + pneumonia_imgs

    plt.figure(figsize=(12,6))
    for i, f in enumerate(samples):
        with zipf.open(f) as file:
            img = Image.open(file)
            plt.subplot(2,3,i+1)
            plt.imshow(img, cmap='gray')
            plt.title(f.split('/')[-2])
            plt.axis('off')
    plt.show()
In [7]:
#Detect corrupted images
corrupted = []
with ZipFile(zip_path, 'r') as zipf:
    for f in zipf.namelist():
        if f.lower().endswith(('.jpeg','.jpg','.png')):
            try:
                with zipf.open(f) as file:
                    img = Image.open(file)
                    img.verify()
            except:
                corrupted.append(f)
print("Corrupted images:", corrupted)

Corrupted images: []
In [8]:
colors = {'NORMAL':'blue', 'PNEUMONIA':'red'}

with ZipFile(zip_path, 'r') as zipf:
    normal_file = normal_imgs[0]
    pneumo_file = pneumonia_imgs[0]

    for f,label in [(normal_file,'NORMAL'), (pneumo_file,'PNEUMONIA')]:
        with zipf.open(f) as file:
            img = np.array(Image.open(file).convert('L'))
            plt.hist(img.ravel(), bins=256, color=colors[label], alpha=0.5, label=label)

plt.legend()
plt.title("Pixel intensity histogram")
plt.show()

#Aspect ratio analysis
ratios = []
with ZipFile(zip_path, 'r') as zipf:
    for f in zipf.namelist():
        if f.lower().endswith(('.jpeg','.jpg','.png')):
            with zipf.open(f) as file:
                img = Image.open(file)
                ratios.append(img.width / img.height)
print("Aspect ratio stats: min =", np.min(ratios), ", max =", np.max(ratios), ", mean =", np.mean(ratios))

In [10]:
#Detect near-blank images
small_or_blank = []
with ZipFile(zip_path, 'r') as zipf:
    for f in zipf.namelist():
        if f.lower().endswith(('.jpeg','.jpg','.png')):
            with zipf.open(f) as file:
                img = np.array(Image.open(file).convert('L'))
                if np.mean(img) < 5:  # nearly black
                    small_or_blank.append(f)
print("Near-blank images:", small_or_blank)

#Brightness analysis per class
brightness = []
with ZipFile(zip_path,'r') as zipf:
    for f in zipf.namelist():
        if f.lower().endswith(('.jpeg','.jpg','.png')):
            label = 'NORMAL' if 'normal' in f.lower() else 'PNEUMONIA'
            with zipf.open(f) as file:
                img = np.array(Image.open(file).convert('L'))
                brightness.append({'label':label,'mean_intensity':img.mean()})
brightness_df = pd.DataFrame(brightness)

plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
sns.boxplot(x='label', y='mean_intensity', data=brightness_df)
plt.title("Average pixel intensity per class")
plt.show()

Chest X-ray Dataset – Data Exploration¶

1. Dataset Characteristics¶

The dataset contains chest X-ray images classified into two classes: NORMAL and PNEUMONIA, split into train, validation, and test sets.

Subset NORMAL PNEUMONIA
Train 1341 3875
Validation 8 8
Test 146 390

Observations:

  • The training set is highly imbalanced (~3:1 ratio of PNEUMONIA:NORMAL).
  • The validation set is very small (16 images total), which may not reliably evaluate model performance.

2. Data Quality Issues¶

  • Corrupted images: None detected.
  • Near-blank images: None detected.
  • Variable image sizes and aspect ratios:
    • Widths and heights vary significantly (e.g., 800×600 to 3000×2500 pixels).
    • Aspect ratios mostly range between 1.2 and 1.5.
    • Resizing and standardization are required before training.
  • Pixel intensity / brightness differences:
    • Histograms and boxplots show variation between NORMAL and PNEUMONIA images.

3.Data preprocessing and feature engineering¶

In [13]:
from tensorflow.keras.preprocessing.image import ImageDataGenerator
# Image dimensions for CNN
IMG_HEIGHT = 224
IMG_WIDTH = 224
BATCH_SIZE = 16
In [14]:
# Training data generator with augmentation
train_datagen = ImageDataGenerator(
    rescale=1./255,               # Normalize pixels
    rotation_range=15,             # Rotate ±15 degrees
    width_shift_range=0.1,         # Shift horizontally
    height_shift_range=0.1,        # Shift vertically
    zoom_range=0.1,                # Random zoom
    horizontal_flip=True,          # Flip horizontally
    fill_mode='nearest',           # Fill empty pixels
    validation_split=0.0           # Validation handled separately
)
In [15]:
# Validation / Test generator (no augmentation)
test_val_datagen = ImageDataGenerator(
    rescale=1./255
)
In [16]:
import os, shutil, random

src = "/content/extracted_equalized"
base_dir = "/content/data/Chest-Xray"

# Define split ratios
split = {"train":0.7, "val":0.2, "test":0.1}

# Create folders
for s in split:
    for cls in ["NORMAL","PNEUMONIA"]:
        os.makedirs(os.path.join(base_dir, s, cls), exist_ok=True)

# Distribute images
for f in os.listdir(src):
    if f.lower().endswith((".png",".jpg",".jpeg")):
        cls = "NORMAL" if "NORMAL" in f.upper() else "PNEUMONIA"
        r = random.random()
        if r < split["train"]:
            target = os.path.join(base_dir, "train", cls)
        elif r < split["train"] + split["val"]:
            target = os.path.join(base_dir, "val", cls)
        else:
            target = os.path.join(base_dir, "test", cls)
        shutil.copy(os.path.join(src,f), os.path.join(target,f))

print("✅ Train/validation/test folders ready")

✅ Train/validation/test folders ready

    os.path.join(base_dir,"train"),
    target_size=(IMG_HEIGHT, IMG_WIDTH),
    batch_size=BATCH_SIZE,
    class_mode='binary',
    color_mode='rgb',
    shuffle=True
)

    class_mode='binary',
    color_mode='rgb',

)

    target_size=(IMG_HEIGHT, IMG_WIDTH),

In [18]:
from sklearn.utils.class_weight import compute_class_weight
import numpy as np

# Get class indices
classes = list(train_generator.class_indices.keys())
y_train = train_generator.classes

# Compute class weights
class_weights = compute_class_weight(
    class_weight='balanced',
    classes=np.unique(y_train),
    y=y_train
)

class_weights = dict(enumerate(class_weights))
print("Class weights:", class_weights)

Class weights: {0: np.float64(3.2384526558891453), 1: np.float64(0.591292430950875)}
In [19]:
import cv2

def equalize_image(img):
    img = cv2.cvtColor(img, cv2.COLOR_RGB2GRAY)
    img = cv2.equalizeHist(img)
    img = cv2.cvtColor(img, cv2.COLOR_GRAY2RGB)
    return img

Summary of Preprocessing & Feature Engineering¶

I Resize all images to 224×224 (or any fixed size for CNN).

Normalize pixel values to 0–1.

Convert to 3-channel RGB for pretrained CNNs.

Apply data augmentation to training set.

Handle class imbalance with class weights or oversampling.

Optional: Histogram equalization for better contrast.

4. A model training and evaluation¶

Model Choice and Learning Objectives¶

For my project, I chose DenseNet121, a pre-trained convolutional neural network (CNN).

Why I chose DenseNet121:

  • It is pre-trained on ImageNet, so it already understands general image features like edges, textures, and shapes.
  • It is efficient and accurate, which makes it suitable for small medical datasets like chest X-rays.
  • Its dense connections allow feature reuse, helping the model learn effectively even with limited data.

Why I used Transfer Learning:

  • Transfer learning lets me reuse knowledge from a model trained on a large dataset for my task.
  • I froze the base layers of DenseNet121 and trained only the top layers on my chest X-ray dataset.
  • Later, I can optionally fine-tune some deeper layers to improve accuracy.
  • This approach saves training time while boosting performance.

What I expect to learn:

  • How to classify chest X-rays as normal or pneumonia.
  • How to adapt a pre-trained CNN for medical image analysis.
  • How to evaluate the model using accuracy and AUC.
  • How to visualize predictions using Grad-CAM heatmaps to see which regions influence the model’s decisions.

In summary, using DenseNet121 with transfer learning allows me to train a high-performing model efficiently and gain insights into how CNNs interpret medical images.

In [22]:
# -----------------------------
#  Imports
# -----------------------------
import tensorflow as tf
from tensorflow.keras.applications import DenseNet121
from tensorflow.keras.layers import GlobalAveragePooling2D, Dropout, Dense
from tensorflow.keras.models import Model
from tensorflow.keras.optimizers import Adam
from tensorflow.keras.callbacks import ModelCheckpoint, EarlyStopping, ReduceLROnPlateau
from tensorflow.keras.preprocessing.image import ImageDataGenerator

# -----------------------------
# Parameters
# -----------------------------
IMG_HEIGHT = 224
IMG_WIDTH = 224
BATCH_SIZE = 16
EPOCHS = 10

# -----------------------------
#  Data Generators
# -----------------------------
train_datagen = ImageDataGenerator(
    rescale=1./255,
    rotation_range=15,
    width_shift_range=0.1,
    height_shift_range=0.1,
    zoom_range=0.1,
    horizontal_flip=True
)

val_test_datagen = ImageDataGenerator(rescale=1./255)

train_generator = train_datagen.flow_from_directory(
    '/content/data/Chest-Xray/train',
    target_size=(IMG_HEIGHT, IMG_WIDTH),
    batch_size=BATCH_SIZE,
    class_mode='binary',
    color_mode='rgb'
)

val_generator = val_test_datagen.flow_from_directory(
    '/content/data/Chest-Xray/val',
    target_size=(IMG_HEIGHT, IMG_WIDTH),
    batch_size=BATCH_SIZE,
    class_mode='binary',
    color_mode='rgb'
)

# -----------------------------
#  Class weights (if needed)
# -----------------------------
class_weight = {0: 3.24, 1: 0.59}  # adjust based on your dataset

# -----------------------------
#  Build DenseNet121 Model
# -----------------------------
base_model = DenseNet121(weights='imagenet', include_top=False,
                         input_shape=(IMG_HEIGHT, IMG_WIDTH, 3))
base_model.trainable = False
x = base_model.output
x = GlobalAveragePooling2D()(x)
x = Dropout(0.5)(x)
predictions = Dense(1, activation='sigmoid')(x)

model = Model(inputs=base_model.input, outputs=predictions)

model.compile(
    optimizer=Adam(learning_rate=1e-4),
    loss='binary_crossentropy',
    metrics=['accuracy', tf.keras.metrics.AUC(name='auc')]
)

print("✅ Model ready for training.")

# -----------------------------
#  Callbacks
# -----------------------------
checkpoint = ModelCheckpoint(
    filepath="/content/densenet121.weights.h5",
    save_weights_only=True,
    save_best_only=True,
    monitor='val_auc',
    mode='max'
)

early_stop = EarlyStopping(monitor='val_auc', patience=3, mode='max', restore_best_weights=True)
reduce_lr = ReduceLROnPlateau(monitor='val_auc', factor=0.2, patience=2, mode='max')

# -----------------------------
# Train the Model
# -----------------------------
history = model.fit(
    train_generator,
    validation_data=val_generator,
    epochs=EPOCHS,
    class_weight=class_weight,
    callbacks=[checkpoint, early_stop, reduce_lr]
)

# -----------------------------
#  Evaluate on Test Set
# -----------------------------
test_generator = val_test_datagen.flow_from_directory(
    '/content/data/Chest-Xray/test',
    target_size=(IMG_HEIGHT, IMG_WIDTH),
    batch_size=BATCH_SIZE,
    class_mode='binary',
    color_mode='rgb',
    shuffle=False
)

loss, acc, auc = model.evaluate(test_generator)
print(f"📊 Test Accuracy: {acc*100:.2f}%, Test AUC: {auc:.4f}")

Found 5609 images belonging to 2 classes.
Found 2818 images belonging to 2 classes.

Do not pass these arguments to `fit()`, as they will be ignored.

Found 1549 images belonging to 2 classes.
97/97 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 247s 3s/step - accuracy: 0.7495 - auc: 0.6614 - loss: 0.5712
📊 Test Accuracy: 77.40%, Test AUC: 0.8977

Results¶

The DenseNet121 model was trained for 10 epochs using transfer learning.
Key results are summarized below:

  • Training Accuracy: ~75%
  • Validation Accuracy: ~76–77%
  • Test Accuracy: 77.4%
  • Validation AUC: 0.8978
  • Test AUC: 0.8977

The AUC values (~0.90) indicate that the model has strong discriminative power in distinguishing between pneumonia and normal chest X-rays.
The accuracy is consistent across training, validation, and test sets, suggesting that the model generalizes well and does not overfit.

In [37]:
# Plot training & validation accuracy, loss, AUC
def plot_training_curves(history):
    acc = history.history['accuracy']
    val_acc = history.history['val_accuracy']
    loss = history.history['loss']
    val_loss = history.history['val_loss']
    auc = history.history['auc']
    val_auc = history.history['val_auc']
    epochs = range(1, len(acc) + 1)

    plt.figure(figsize=(15, 5))

    # Accuracy
    plt.subplot(1, 3, 1)
    plt.plot(epochs, acc, 'b-', label='Training Acc')
    plt.plot(epochs, val_acc, 'r-', label='Validation Acc')
    plt.title('Accuracy')
    plt.legend()

    # Loss
    plt.subplot(1, 3, 2)
    plt.plot(epochs, loss, 'b-', label='Training Loss')
    plt.plot(epochs, val_loss, 'r-', label='Validation Loss')
    plt.title('Loss')
    plt.legend()

    # AUC
    plt.subplot(1, 3, 3)
    plt.plot(epochs, auc, 'b-', label='Training AUC')
    plt.plot(epochs, val_auc, 'r-', label='Validation AUC')
    plt.title('AUC')
    plt.legend()

    plt.show()

# Call function
plot_training_curves(history)

Training & Validation Curves¶

  • Accuracy: Training rises from ~50% → 75%; validation closely follows → good learning.
  • Loss: Decreases steadily; validation slightly higher → minimal overfitting.
  • AUC: Training ~0.84, Validation ~0.90 → strong ability to distinguish NORMAL vs PNEUMONIA.
In [32]:
#Grad-CAM Visualizations
import random
import os
from tensorflow.keras.applications.densenet import preprocess_input

# Directory containing test images
test_dir = "/content/data/Chest-Xray/test"

# Pick 5 random test images
all_test_images = []
for root, dirs, files in os.walk(test_dir):
    for file in files:
        if file.endswith(".jpeg") or file.endswith(".jpg") or file.endswith(".png"):
            all_test_images.append(os.path.join(root, file))

random_images = random.sample(all_test_images, 5)

# Generate Grad-CAM for 5 random test images
for img_path in random_images:
    print(f"🔍 Showing Grad-CAM for: {img_path}")
    display_gradcam(img_path, model, preprocess_input)

Grad-CAM Visualizations¶

To further interpret the model’s predictions, I generated Grad-CAM heatmaps on several test X-ray images. These heatmaps highlight the regions in the image that contributed most to the model’s decision.

In pneumonia cases, the model’s attention (red regions) was focused on the lungs, particularly where opacities and abnormalitiesare visible.

The heatmaps confirm that the model is learning medically relevant features rather than relying on irrelevant background areas.

Some examples show that the model sometimes highlights edges or regions outside the lungs. This may explain certain misclassifications and suggests that more data or fine-tuning could improve focus.

These visualizations demonstrate that the model is not a black box — it is actually “looking” at the same areas a radiologist would examine when diagnosing pneumonia.

5.Live Demo of AI Pneumonia Detector:¶

To demonstrate the functionality of the trained DenseNet121 model, we implemented a live demo that randomly selects test chest X-ray images and predicts whether each image is Normal or Pneumonia.

Predictions are displayed above each X-ray image, with color coding:

🟢 Normal – can be reviewed later.

🔴 Pneumonia – flagged immediately for radiologist review.

This demo simulates a real-time triage system, highlighting how the AI model can assist radiologists by prioritizing high-risk cases.

In [36]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from PIL import Image
import numpy as np
import random
import time
from tensorflow.keras.preprocessing.image import img_to_array

# Parameters
IMG_HEIGHT = 224
IMG_WIDTH = 224
DELAY = 2  # seconds

# Load random test images
random_images = random.sample(all_test_images, 5)

def preprocess_image(image_path):
    img = Image.open(image_path).convert('RGB').resize((IMG_WIDTH, IMG_HEIGHT))
    img_array = img_to_array(img) / 255.0
    return np.expand_dims(img_array, axis=0)

def predict_label(image_path):
    img_array = preprocess_image(image_path)
    pred = model.predict(img_array, verbose=0)[0][0]
    if pred >= 0.5:
        return "🔴 Pneumonia", "red"
    else:
        return "🟢 Normal", "green"

# Display images with predictions
for image_path in random_images:
    label, color = predict_label(image_path)
    img = Image.open(image_path)
    plt.imshow(img, cmap='gray')
    plt.title(label, color=color, fontsize=16)
    plt.axis('off')
    plt.show()
    time.sleep(DELAY)  # pause between images

6.Final discussion on pipeline¶

After completing training and evaluation of the DenseNet121 model, I observed several important points:

Strengths¶

  • High AUC (~0.89): The model is able to reliably distinguish between pneumonia and normal cases.
  • Grad-CAM Visualizations: Heatmaps confirm that the model focuses on lung regions with visible abnormalities, which aligns with medical reasoning.
  • Transfer Learning Benefits: By using DenseNet121 (pretrained on ImageNet), the model achieved strong performance without requiring an extremely large dataset.

Limitations¶

  • Moderate Accuracy (~77%): While good, accuracy leaves room for improvement, especially for critical medical applications.
  • Occasional Misclassifications: Grad-CAM shows that in some cases, the model attends to irrelevant areas (e.g., edges of the X-ray), which may lead to errors.
  • Training Time: DenseNet121 is computationally heavy, making training relatively slow compared to simpler CNNs.

Future Work¶

  • Fine-tuning More Layers: Unfreezing deeper DenseNet layers could improve feature learning.
  • Data Augmentation: Increasing variability in training data (rotations, brightness changes, etc.) may improve generalization.
  • Ensembling Models: Combining DenseNet with other architectures could further boost performance.
  • Larger Dataset: Adding more chest X-ray images would reduce overfitting and improve robustness.

Conclusion¶

In this project, I successfully implemented a DenseNet121 model with transfer learning to classify chest X-ray images into pneumonia and normal cases.

The model achieved:

  • Test Accuracy: ~77.4%
  • Test AUC: ~0.8977

Grad-CAM visualizations confirmed that the model focused on medically relevant lung regions, making its predictions interpretable. While the model performs well, there is room for improvement through fine-tuning, data augmentation, and larger datasets. Overall, this project demonstrates the effectiveness of transfer learning for medical image classification and provides a strong foundation for future research.
